1 Overview
With the emergence of cheap computation, ubiquitous sensor coverage, and new
data processing technologies, a new view that encompasses all concepts is neces-
sary to highlight the possible synergies among the individual technological solutions
and approaches. For this purpose, a view that focuses on data is the most suitable.
A general data awareness and a data-driven culture has to be adopted in order to
capitalize on the different technological solutions.
The main concept unifying data-driven technologies is the observation —> ori-
entation —> decision —> action loop (or any variant of these), which also underlies
many methodical, human, and organizational activities. (For more information, see
Wikipedia - OODA Loop.)
Figure 1.1: OODA Loop
In organizational context, the primary goal is to base the activities on systemati-
cally collected and organized data in order to enable efﬁcient, factual, and data-driven
decisions and operations.

Although big data is the most frequently discussed topic in the domain of data man-
agement, data management solutions have to be considered in their complexity. The
heart of a data-driven mode of operation is choosing multiple, adequate, and mu-
tually supporting data storage and retrieval technologies. However, the decision is
obscured by the much used "SQL vs. NoSQL " phraseology. In reality, the choice is not
exclusively between SQL or NoSQL as the question is more complex. A data ecosys-
tem is needed that is built out of multiple data solutions that are together appropriate
for transactional and analytical purposes.

1.3 Blockchain or Distributed Ledger Technologies
In case of multi-party transactions, agreement and consensus, so in this sense data
validity, is crucial. In this regard, the biggest challenges arise when it comes to ﬁ-
nancial transactions, where the common consensus between the parties—having
in some cases opposing interests—has to be ensured by all means. Therefore, it
is very natural that distributed ledger or consensus technologies emerged in the ﬁ-
nancial domain. (Consider cryptocurrencies that are prime examples of blockchain
usage.) But this set of solutions has much broader application in case of secure data
exchange and multi-party computation.
1.4 Conclusion
As we can see, data serves as the main "raw material" and "product" in all of these
processes, so we have to dwell deeper into understanding what formats data can
take.

2 What Is Data?
Dictionaries deﬁne data as facts, numbers, statistics, or measurements that are gath-
ered by means of observation. 123 The word “data” comes form Latin: it is the plural
form, and its singular is “datum”. Being originally a plural noun, "data" should take
plural verbs if an author is following a more traditional style. But, over the course of
the time, "data" has become separated from its Latin origins. Now, it is also recog-
nized as an uncountable noun (just like "information") in the English language and
takes singular verbs and modiﬁers. 4
"Data" and "information" are used as synonyms in everyday life. Although, in cer-
tain sources, it is stated that the two words are not synonymous. The explanation is
that data is collected in order to obtain information from it. Data can be considered
the raw material—a pile of disorganized, random, and useless facts—that is after be-
ing processed becomes valuable information, which can support decision-making.

that present the information as graphs, diagrams, tables, etc. therefore helping or-

3 Sources of Data
The ultimate fate of data is to end up on a storage medium. These storage media
or locations from where data is being accessed or used are called data sources in
the context of computer science.10 Some of these mediums have ﬁle system access
patterns (for example, local hard drives in laptops and computers), while other stor-
age solutions offer other abstraction (for example, key-value APIs). It is also very
popular to store data in databases, which have even more sophisticated interfaces.
In order to familiarize ourselves a bit more with the world of data management,
let us take a look at some easy examples of data storage.
3.1 File Systems and Files
File systems offer a simple path and ﬁle extension based solution for storing data.
Most common formats are text, images, videos, etc.
Example: C:\User\jane\cv.txt
In this example, the ﬁrst part of the path is the folder where the ﬁle resides ( C:

The simple text ﬁle is one of the fundamental data storage ﬁle formats. This is a
typical human-readable format, meaning that the data is represented as text, en-
coded with the binary representation of a symbol set from a natural, human lan-
guage.

Although it is very tempting to consider this format to be perfectly raw, but this is
not the case. This is just plain, unformatted text.
The text format is designed to be humanly readable, which means that we have
to encode the character set of a certain human language and store them in a bi-
nary format. In the early days of computing, this caused considerable confusion
and problems—even in such a basic case, as marking the languages in a coherent
way (see the ISO/IEC 10646 standard)—but with the advent of Unicode ®, it became
much less problematic.
The Unicode Standard is a character encoding standard for computer processing
that is used for text representation. By bringing order to a chaotic situation that
has made international text ﬁle interchange challenging, the Unicode Standard
establishes a uniform method of encoding multilingual plain text. It considerably
makes the job easier of users who work with multilingual text and frequently em-
ploy mathema

3.1.2 CSV
The comma-separated values (CSV) ﬁle is a special type of text ﬁle: it is plain text
delimited format. Delimited means that a special character, or delimiter, is used to
divide data into ﬁelds in a line. In a CSV ﬁle, each line corresponds to a data record,
and each record has one or more ﬁelds, which are separated by commas. The CSV
ﬁle is very popular and reliable format despite the fact that it is not a formal stan-
dard. This format is frequently used for exporting data from a spreadsheet to an-
other application. The key rules of the CSV format are the following:
• Comma-delimited: A comma separates each ﬁeld.
• Double quotation marks are unique: Double quotation marks must be used
around ﬁelds that include commas.

3.1.3 JSON
JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) is a standardized and language-independent
data exchange format that is easy to handle for both humans and machines. 13
JSON, as its name suggests, comes from JavaScript being based on JavaScript
object literals. However, there is no need to know JavaScript to understand JSON
because it is a language-independent format that is used for storing data and not
for programming.

ferent platforms and systems. Data exchange is critical for many businesses and
organizations, so stand-alone systems must be able to communicate and inter-

patible with numerous programming languages. Therefore, it can act as a "transla-
tor" between system that "speak" different languages. 14

ordered list.15 3.1.3.1 shows some personal data of an employee. For example,

5 Data Management
After we gained some experience in handling data itself, we have to turn our atten-
tion to a more systematic approach of handling data, namely, data management.
Data management is the application of discipline and control to achieve a consis-
tent, trusted, secure and shared representation of data created, collected or gener-
ated by an organization. This is often referenced as a "single version of truth".
The concept of data management arose in the 1980s as technology moved from
sequential processing (ﬁrst punched cards, then magnetic tape) to random access
storage.
The original constraints of data storage media, namely their sequential nature en-
forced some hard limitation on how much data one could feasibly store and in
what order one could access it (think: tape medium, had to "seek" to the right part
of a sequence and linearly read from there). With the advent of "random access"
media (think: hard drives) it became possible to access individual snippets of data

Document: DMD
Page: 24
ium, had to "seek" to the right part
of a sequence and linearly read from there). With the advent of "random access"
media (think: hard drives) it became possible to access individual snippets of data
/ facts irrespective of their storage order, so this greatly enhanced query efﬁciency.
Since read operations were no longer prohibitively expensive to carry out (in com-
putation), generally, much more storage capacity had been utilized (namely: if you
don’t have to "seek over" a lot of data, it is more tempting to store additional things).
This also added to the general shift, where data was no longer only dealt with in the
way and format required by a given process, but became a generally useful entity
to be accessed and utilized by multiple processes. Thus data management was
born.

5.1.1 Lambda architecture
Lambda architecture is a design patter, that tries to mitigate the discrepancies be-
tween the needs for a long term, structured storage solution and the fact, that data
has to be up to data, as well as arrives in potentially large quantities, quickly. These
two perspectives often require different technologies, thus can have contradictory
requirements.
The main concept behind lambda architecture is that of uniﬁcation, solving this
tension by having two data sources that are directly exposed to data consumers
(like business analysts). One data source is the highly optimized long term storage
the other one is responsible for processing the "novelty" that arrives to the data
systems currently. The two data sets are joined together to make it look like one,
so the analysts themselves a

6.3.1 Database transactions
A database transaction is a unit of work performed within a process against a
database, running in isolation from other similar processes. A transaction repre-
sents a change (addition, subtraction or mutation of data) in a database.
Fig 6.2.1: Database transaction
Transactions in a database environment have two main purposes:
- To provide reliable units of work that allow correct recovery from failures and
keep a database consistent even in cases of system failure
- To provide isolation between programs accessing a database concurrently
In a database management system, a transaction is a single unit of work, some-
times made up of multiple operations. Any logical calculation done in a consistent
mode in a database is known as a transaction.
44

Document: DMD

One example is a transfer from one bank account to another: the complete trans-
action requires subtracting the amount to be transferred from one account and
adding that same amount to the other. In case this transaction runs in a single
database the addition and subtraction happens in a single transaction. This also
means that other transaction that would affect the involved bank accounts must
wait until the transaction completes.
A database transaction, by deﬁnition, must be atomic (it must either ﬁnished com-
pletely or have no effect at all, no partial writes), consistent (it must conform to ex-
isting constraints in the database), isolated (it must not affect other transactions)
and durable (it must get written to persistent storage). In database terminology
these properties often mentioned as the acronym ACID.

In computer science, ACID (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability) is a set of
properties of database transactions intended to guarantee data validity despite er-
rors, power failures, and other mishaps. In the context of databases, a sequence of
database operations that satisﬁes the ACID properties (which can be perceived as

earlier work by Jim Gray, who named atomicity, consistency, and durability, but not

A NoSQL (originally referring to "non-SQL " or "non-relational") database provides a
mechanism for storage and retrieval of data that is modeled in means other than
the tabular relations used in relational databases.
Such databases have existed since the late 1960s, but the name "NoSQL " was only
coined in the early 21st century, triggered by the needs of Web 2.0 companies,
especially Google and Amazon. NoSQL databases are increasingly used in big
data and real-time web applications. NoSQL systems are also sometimes called
"Not only SQL " to emphasize that they may support SQL-like query languages or sit
alongside SQL databases in polyglot-persistent architectures.
The naming is a bit unfortunate, implying that the challenge is with the query lan-
guage (SQL) that has to be changed. The reality is that these databases challenge
the ACID compliance part, using the CAP theorem to do so.

6.8 The CAP theorem
In theoretical computer science, the CAP theorem, also named Brewer’s theorem
after computer scientist Eric Brewer, states that it is impossible for a distributed
data store to simultaneously provide more than two out of the following three guar-
antees:
- Consistency: Every read receives the most recent write or an error
- Availability: Every request receives a (non-error) response, without the guar-
antee that it contains the most recent write
- Partition tolerance: The system continues to operate despite an arbitrary
number of messages being dropped (or delayed) by the network between
nodes
When a network partition failure happens, it must be decided whether to:
- Cancel the operation and thus decrease the availability but ensure consis-
tency or to
- Proceed with the operation and thus provide availability but risk inconsis-
tency
The CAP theorem implies that in the

tency or to
- Proceed with the operation and thus provide availability but risk inconsis-
tency
The CAP theorem implies that in the presence of a network partition, one has to
choose between consistency and availability. Note that consistency as deﬁned
in the CAP theorem is quite different from the consistency guaranteed in ACID
database transactions. Eric Brewer argues that the often-used "two out of three"
concept can be somewhat misleading because system designers only need to sac-
riﬁce consistency or availability in the presence of partitions, and that in many sys-
tems partitions are rare.

Motivations for this approach include simplicity of design, simpler "horizontal"
scaling to clusters of machines (which is a problem for relational databases), ﬁner
control over availability and limiting the object-relational impedance mismatch.
The data structures used by NoSQL databases (e.g. key–value pair, wide column,
graph, or document) are different from those used by default in relational databases,
making some operations faster in NoSQL. The particular suitability of a given NoSQL
database depends on the problem it must solve. Sometimes the data structures
used by NoSQL databases are also viewed as "more ﬂexible" than relational database
tables.
Many NoSQL stores compromise consistency (in the sense of the CAP theorem) in
favor of availability, partition tolerance, and speed. Barriers to the greater adoption
of NoSQL stores include the use of low-level query languages (instead of SQL, for
instance), lack of ability to perform ad hoc joins across tables, lack of standardized

NoSQL stores lack true ACID transactions, although a few databases have made
them central to their designs.
Instead, most NoSQL databases offer a concept of "eventual consistency", in which
database changes are propagated to all nodes "eventually" (typically within mil-
liseconds), so queries for data might not return updated data immediately or might
result in reading data that is not accurate, a problem known as stale reads.
Additionally, some NoSQL systems may exhibit lost writes and other forms of data
loss. Some NoSQL systems provide concepts such as write-ahead logging to avoid

7.5 Model types
7.5.1 Conceptual data model
This is the highest level ER model in that it contains the least granular detail but
establishes the overall scope of what is to be included within the model set. The
conceptual ER model normally deﬁnes master reference data entities that are com-
monly used by the organization. Developing an enterprise-wide conceptual ER
model is useful to support documenting the data architecture for an organization.
A conceptual ER model may be used as the foundation for one or more logical data
models (see below). The purpose of the conceptual ER model is then to establish
structural metadata commonality for the master data entities between the set of

models (see below). The purpose of the conceptual ER model is then to establish
structural metadata commonality for the master data entities between the set of
logical ER models. The conceptual data model may be used to form commonality
relationships between ER models as a basis for data model integration.
7.5.2 Logical data model
A logical ER model does not require a conceptual ER model, especially if the scope
of the logical ER model includes only the development of a distinct information
system. The logical ER model contains more detail than the conceptual ER model.
In addition to master data entities, operational and transactional data entities are
now deﬁned. The details of each data entity are developed and the relationships
between these data entities are established. The logical ER model is however de-
veloped independently of the speciﬁc database management system into which it
can be implemented.

yields the best performance and integrity. In 1970 the First Normal Form was de-

days, there are some additional considerations that differ from those in 1970, be-

An SQL join clause – corresponding to a join operation in relational algebra – com-

The main (though not the only) query language for Neo4j is the graph speciﬁc Cypher.
Though Neo4j is a schemaless database, one can use key: value style attributes for
any attached to any primitives - namely nodes and edges, thus forming a "property
graph".
Without any schema, the database only contains the data itself, thus the CREATE
statements are also interpreted to create primitives.

Afterwards we looked for a Person type entity having a given attribute, and returned
the full object as a result. This a typical starting point for a lookup / traversal.

12 Computation and it’ s trade-offs
Whenever we think of the progress in the "power" of IT systems (whatever that may
mean), we visualize a steady, or even an exponential march towards some inﬁnite
horizon. The often quoted Moore’s law is one example.
Fig 9.1: Illustration of Moore’s law on logarithmic scale
Though in one sense, these are valid intuitions, since they cover the amazing progress
we made in the last 70-80 years, they obscure some complexities that hind behind
this movement.
"Computation" as we execute it on any given hardware can be decomposed to at
least three interacting elements, that each have a crucial say in the ﬁnal perfor-
mance we can achieve, namely:
101

Document: DMD
Page: 103
- Single thread computation power
- Communication speed
- Storage capacity (and speed)
All of these components have a deﬁning inﬂuence on the ﬁnal performance on a
given system / workload, and are often interchangeable to each-other.
12.0.0.1 Trading computation for communication
There are hard physical limits on how fast a single computational unit can get, and
although researchers were able to make progress in the ﬁeld for a long time, we are
nearing the hard limits.
Though there is no necessary linear relationship between "clock speed", that is,
the base instruction execution frequency of a processing unit and it’s total "perfor-
mance", it is instructive to see, how clock speed plateaued somewhere in the mid
2000s.
Fig 9.2: Illustration of measured CPU clock speeds in MHz
But if we are having problems with speeding up single instruction execution, what
can we do? Well, the observation that was crucial in breaking this barrier (and un-
leashed the driving force behind contemporary a

Document: DMD
Page: 103
 having problems with speeding up single instruction execution, what
can we do? Well, the observation that was crucial in breaking this barrier (and un-
leashed the driving force behind contemporary advancements in computation) is
that many workloads can be parallelizable, that is, we can use multiple computa-
tional units to work on them in parallel, and then unify their results. This is in fact
the cause for the emergence of multi-core CPUs as well as the recent success of
GPUs.
102

Document: DMD
Page: 104
However there are limits to this type of processing. Not every part of every algo-
rithm is equally parallelizable, and even in "nicely behaving" cases, there are parts
of computation that require to be executed serially. Amdahl’s law tells us, that the
total speedup that we can expect in "wall clock time" is hard constrained by the se-
quential part, so whatever we do, however many execution "threads" we have, we
won’t be able to get faster than the serial execution of the part that requires such
treatment.
103

Document: DMD
Page: 105
Fig 9.3: Illustration of Amdahl’s law
But a careful formulation of Amdahl’s law also reveals, that there is also a hidden
cost to parallelization, namely: communication. In case of a parallel workload we
at minimum have to get the tasks distributed to the given processing units and the
results collected back (for every parallelizable portion), so if the transfer of data be-
tween the execution units takes more time than the execution itself, we can end up
in the situation, that our parallel execution will run slower than the original single
thread mode. The takeaway is twofold: 1. There exist a tradeoff between commu-
nication and computation. 2. Since communication is oftentimes orders of magni-
tude slower than computation, this can form a strong bottleneck.

We have to always bear in mind, that the principle of communication-computation
tradeoff exist not just in case of single CPUs or computers, but also in case of mul-
tiple machine distributed systems.

Though the exact description of the phenomena exceeds the scope of this discus-
sion, we have to note, that there is a fundamental trade-off between the potentially
multiple or "renewed" calculation of some results versus their storage and retrieval.
Let us take the example of caching. If we know, that we have to use a given value
for computation many times after each-other, it is very wise to store this value in a
temporary storage and only do a "lookup".

13 Distributed Systems
Distributed systems were popular before the emergence of cloud computing, but
mostly accessible for large organizations. The ﬁrst conference was held in 1985
using the name Workshop on Distributed Algorithms on Graphs . It was later
renamed to International Symposium on Distributed Computing (DISC) .
The subjects discussed during these conferences included consensus algorithms,
scalability topics for networks and storage.
Distributed computing is a ﬁeld of computer science that studies distributed
systems. A distributed system is a system whose components are located on
different networked computers, which communicate and coordinate their actions
by passing messages to one another from any system. The components interact
with one another in order to achieve a common goal. Three signiﬁcant
characteristics of distributed systems are: concurrency of components, lack of a
global clock, and independent failure of components.

13.1 Service-oriented architecture
Service-oriented architecture (SOA) is an architectural style that supports service

where services are provided to the other components by application components,
through a communication protocol over a network. A service is a discrete unit of
functionality that can be accessed remotely and acted upon and updated
independently, such as retrieving a credit card statement online. SOA is also
intended to be independent of vendors, products and technologies.
Service orientation is a way of thinking in terms of services and service-based
development and the outcomes of services.

A service has four properties according to one of many deﬁnitions of SOA:

Taylor and Larry Roberts developed ARPANET (Advanced Research
Projects Agency Networks) and, eventually, became the precursor of

17.8.0.1 BLuetooth Low Energy
Bluetooth Low Energy is a wireless personal area network technology designed
and marketed by the Bluetooth Special Interest Group aimed at novel applications
in the healthcare, ﬁtness, beacons, security, and home entertainment industries.
17.8.0.2 LoRa
LoRa (Long Range) is a proprietary low-power wide-area network modulation
technique. It is based on spread-spectrum modulation techniques derived from
chirp spread spectrum (CSS) technology. It was developed by Cycleo of Grenoble,
France and acquired by Semtech, the founding member of the LoRa Alliance; it is
patented.
17.8.0.3 LAN (Wiﬁ)
A local area network is a computer network that interconnects computers within a
limited area such as a residence, school, laboratory, university campus or ofﬁce
building. By contrast, a wide area network not only covers a larger geographic
distance, but also generally involves leased telecommunication circuits.

17.9 Network Protocols
17.9.0.1 MQTT
MQTT is a lightweight, publish-subscribe network protocol that transports
messages between devices. The protocol usually runs over TCP/IP , however, any
network protocol that provides ordered, lossless, bi-directional connections can
support MQTT . It is designed for connections with remote locations where a
resource constraints exist or the network bandwidth is limited. The protocol is an
open OASIS standard and an ISO recommendation (ISO/IEC 20922).
The MQTT protocol deﬁnes two types of network entities: a message broker and a
number of clients. An MQTT broker is a server that receives all messages from the
clients and then routes the messages to the appropriate destination clients. An
MQTT client is any device (from a micro controller up to a fully-ﬂedged server) that
runs an MQTT library and connects to an MQTT

Document: DMD
Page: 138
nd then routes the messages to the appropriate destination clients. An
MQTT client is any device (from a micro controller up to a fully-ﬂedged server) that
runs an MQTT library and connects to an MQTT broker over a network.
Information is organized in a hierarchy of topics. When a publisher has a new item
of data to distribute, it sends a control message with the data to the connected
broker. The broker then distributes the information to any clients that have
subscribed to that topic. The publisher does not need to have any data on the
number or locations of subscribers, and subscribers, in turn, do not have to be
conﬁgured with any data about the publishers.
137

Document: DMD

If a broker receives a message on a topic for which there are no current
subscribers, the broker discards the message unless the publisher of the message
designated the message as a retained message. A retained message is a normal
MQTT message with the retained ﬂag set to true. The broker stores the last
retained message and the corresponding QoS for the selected topic. Each client
that subscribes to a topic pattern that matches the topic of the retained message
receives the retained message immediately after they subscribe. The broker stores
only one retained message per topic. This allows new subscribers to a topic to
receive the most current value rather than waiting for the next update from a
publisher.
When a publishing client ﬁrst connects to the broker, it can set up a default
message to be sent to subscribers if the broker detects that the publishing client
has unexpectedly disconnected from the broker.
Clients only interact with a broker, but a system may contain several broke

ge to be sent to subscribers if the broker detects that the publishing client
has unexpectedly disconnected from the broker.
Clients only interact with a broker, but a system may contain several broker servers
that exchange data based on their current subscribers’ topics.
A minimal MQTT control message can be as little as two bytes of data. A control
message can carry nearly 256 megabytes of data if needed. There are fourteen
deﬁned message types used to connect and disconnect a client from a broker, to
publish data, to acknowledge receipt of data, and to supervise the connection
between client and server.
MQTT relies on the TCP protocol for data transmission. A variant, MQTT -SN, is
used over other transports such as UDP or Bluetooth.
MQTT sends connection credentials in plain text format and does not include any
measures for security or authentication. This can be provided by using TLS to
encrypt and protect the transferred information against interception, modiﬁcation
or forgery.

17.10 IoT Analytics
IoT Analytics automates the steps required to analyze data from IoT devices. IoT
Analytics ﬁlters, transforms, and enriches IoT data before storing it in a time-series
data store for analysis. You can set up the service to collect only the data you need
from your devices, apply mathematical transformations to process and enrich the
data with device-speciﬁc metadata such as device type and location before storing
it. Then, you can analyze your data by running queries using the SQL or other query
engines, or perform more complex analytics and machine learning inference.
Traditional analytics and business intelligence tools are designed to process
structured data. Raw IoT data often comes from devices that record less
structured data (such as temperature, motion, or sound). As a result the data from
these devices can have signiﬁcant gaps, corrupted messages, and false readings
that must be cleaned up before analysis can occur. Also, IoT data is often only
meaningful in

17.10.2 Process
Data from IoT endpoints usually ends up in a processing service that either
pre-aggregates the data or pushes it to a storage service.
17.10.3 Store
Data coming from the sensors usually ends up on a long term storage that uses
advanced storage optimization techniques to provide cheap and scalable solution
to this problem. There are many options for storing this data that is usually a
time-series.
17.10.3.1 Time-series data stores
A time series database (TSDB) is a software system that is optimized for storing
and serving time series through associated pairs of time(s) and value(s). In some
ﬁelds, time series may be called proﬁles, curves, traces or trends.[2] Several early
time series databases are associated with industrial applications which could
efﬁciently store measured values from sensory equipment (also referred to as data
historians), but now are used in support of a much wider range of applications.

In many cases, the repositories of time-series data will utilize compression

time-series data in many different database types, the design of these systems
with time as a key index is distinctly different from relational databases which
reduce discrete relationships through referential models.
